1:
"And when it was determined that we should sail into Italy, they delivered Paul and certain other prisoners unto one named Julius, a centurion of Augustus' band." The witnesses note the beginning of the voyage to Rome and the providence behind it. Gill notes the voyage was determined by Festus, with the advice of Agrippa and his council, pursuant to Paul's appeal; "and which was founded on the will of God; all which concurred in this affair: it was the decree and will of God that the apostle should go to Rome, which was made known to him." The witnesses note Paul, with other prisoners, delivered to Julius, a centurion, for the voyage to Italy — the human decision and the divine will concurring; the apostle set on his way to Rome by the providence of God. The witnesses note the voyage determined. The witnesses note the will of God.

The witnesses note the voyage to Rome begun — the human decision and the divine will concurring to bring Paul to Rome.

So the witnesses make the verse the beginning of the voyage to Rome. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence of God carrying Paul to Rome, the human decision serving the divine will. The witnesses note the determination: "when it was determined that we should sail into Italy" — the voyage to Italy (Rome) was determined; Gill notes by Festus, with the advice of Agrippa and his council, pursuant to Paul's appeal; the human decision; the journey set. The expositors observe the divine will behind it: Gill notes the determination "was founded on the will of God; all which concurred"; it was the decree and will of God that Paul should go to Rome (Acts 23:11), made known to him; the divine will carrying him; the providence ordering the voyage. The witnesses press the concurrence of the human and divine: the appeal to Caesar, the decision of Festus and Agrippa, and the will of God all concurred to bring Paul to Rome; the human means serving the divine purpose; the providence working through the decisions of men; the will of God accomplished. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence of God: God had purposed that Paul should bear witness at Rome (Acts 23:11), and the providence ordered all — the appeal, the decision, the voyage — to bring it to pass; the sovereign purpose accomplished through human means; "the LORD's counsel, that shall stand" (Proverbs 19:21); the providence directing. The witnesses observe the delivery to Julius: "they delivered Paul... unto one named Julius, a centurion of Augustus' band" — Paul was delivered to a centurion for the voyage; the prisoner in custody, yet on his way to Rome; the apostle borne toward his appointed witness; the providence in the custody. The expositors note the "we" of the narrative: "we should sail" — Luke was with Paul on the voyage; the faithful companion; the eyewitness account; the friend accompanying the apostle. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the voyage determined, the will of God, and the providence carrying Paul to Rome. The verse thus records the beginning of the voyage to Rome — Paul delivered to Julius — teaching the providence of God carrying Paul to Rome, the human decision serving the divine will.
2:
"And entering into a ship of Adramyttium, we launched, meaning to sail by the coasts of Asia; one Aristarchus, a Macedonian of Thessalonica, being with us." The witnesses note the ship and the faithful companion Aristarchus. Gill notes the ship of Adramyttium (a city on the sea shore) was in the port of Caesarea, bound for the coasts of Asia. The witnesses note that Aristarchus, a Macedonian of Thessalonica, accompanied Paul — the faithful friend who shared the apostle's perils (later a fellow-prisoner, Colossians 4:10); the devotion of the companion who would not leave Paul in his bonds and dangers. The witnesses note the ship. The witnesses note the faithful Aristarchus.

The witnesses note the ship and Aristarchus the faithful companion — the friend who shared Paul's perils and would not leave him.

So the witnesses make the verse the ship and the companion Aristarchus. The lesson the expositors draw is the devotion of the faithful companion who shared the apostle's perils. The witnesses note the ship: "entering into a ship of Adramyttium, we launched, meaning to sail by the coasts of Asia" — they boarded a ship of Adramyttium (Gill notes, a city on the sea shore), bound for the coasts of Asia; the first vessel of the voyage; the journey begun. The expositors observe the companion: "one Aristarchus, a Macedonian of Thessalonica, being with us" — Aristarchus, a Macedonian of Thessalonica, accompanied Paul; the faithful friend; the companion of the apostle (Acts 19:29; 20:4); the devoted helper. The witnesses press the devotion of Aristarchus: he accompanied Paul on the perilous voyage, sharing his dangers; later a fellow-prisoner with Paul (Colossians 4:10; Philemon 24); the friend who would not leave the apostle in his bonds and perils; the faithful companion. The expositors draw the lesson on faithful friendship: Aristarchus, by accompanying Paul into danger and bonds, showed the devotion of a true friend; the friend that sticketh closer than a brother (Proverbs 18:24); the love that shares the perils of the persecuted; the faithful companion in trouble. The witnesses observe the comfort of companionship: Paul, a prisoner, was not alone — Luke and Aristarchus were with him; the comfort of faithful friends in trial; the apostle sustained by their company; the fellowship in suffering. The expositors note the providence in the companions: God gave Paul faithful friends to accompany and minister to him; the providence of companionship; the apostle not left desolate; the friends a means of comfort. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ship, the faithful Aristarchus, and the devotion of the companion who shared Paul's perils. The verse thus records the ship and Aristarchus — the faithful companion — teaching the devotion of the friend who shared the apostle's perils and would not leave him.
3:
"And the next day we touched at Sidon. And Julius courteously entreated Paul, and gave him liberty to go unto his friends to refresh himself." The witnesses note the kindness of Julius to Paul. Gill notes Sidon was a famous maritime city in Phoenicia. The witnesses note that Julius the centurion courteously treated Paul, giving him liberty at Sidon to visit his friends and be refreshed — the favour God gave Paul in the eyes of the centurion; the providence raising up kindness for the apostle even in his custody; the humane treatment of the prisoner. The witnesses note the courtesy of Julius. The witnesses note God's favour for Paul.

The witnesses note Julius's kindness to Paul — the favour God gave the apostle in the eyes of his keeper.

So the witnesses make the verse the kindness of Julius to Paul. The lesson the expositors draw is the favour God gave Paul in the eyes of the centurion, the providence raising up kindness for the apostle. The witnesses note the stop at Sidon: "the next day we touched at Sidon" — the ship touched at Sidon (Gill notes, a famous maritime city in Phoenicia); the first stop; the voyage proceeding. The expositors observe the courtesy of Julius: "Julius courteously entreated Paul, and gave him liberty to go unto his friends to refresh himself" — Julius treated Paul kindly, giving him liberty to visit his friends (the Christians at Sidon) and be refreshed; the humane treatment; the kindness of the centurion. The witnesses press the favour God gave Paul: the centurion's kindness was the favour God gave Paul in his eyes; the providence raising up kindness for the apostle even in custody; "he made him to be pitied of all those that carried them captives" (Psalm 106:46); the favour of God; the kindness providential. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence of favour: God gave Paul favour with his keeper, as he had given Joseph favour with the keeper of the prison (Genesis 39:21), and Daniel with the prince of the eunuchs (Daniel 1:9); the providence inclining the hearts of men to kindness; the apostle befriended by his keeper; the favour of God in custody. The witnesses observe the refreshment of the saints: Paul was allowed to visit his friends (the believers at Sidon) and be refreshed; the comfort of the fellowship of the saints; the apostle refreshed by his brethren; the communion of the believers a refreshment. The expositors note the character that won the favour: Julius's kindness was won by Paul's character — the wisdom, dignity, and grace of the apostle; the godly life winning favour; the character commending the man; the favour earned by grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the courtesy of Julius, God's favour for Paul, and the providence raising up kindness. The verse thus records the kindness of Julius — giving Paul liberty to be refreshed — teaching the favour God gave Paul in the eyes of his keeper, the providence raising up kindness for the apostle.
4:
"And when we had launched from thence, we sailed under Cyprus, because the winds were contrary." The witnesses note the difficult sailing against contrary winds. Barnes notes that "sailing under Cyprus" means they sailed along its coast, keeping near it to break off the violent winds; instead of steering a direct course in the open sea (which would expose them to opposing winds), they kept the island between them and the westerly winds, so the force of the wind was broken. The witnesses note the ship sailing under the lee of Cyprus because the winds were contrary — the early difficulty of the voyage, the contrary winds that would mount to the great storm; the trials beginning. The witnesses note the contrary winds. The witnesses note the difficult voyage.

The witnesses note the ship sailing under Cyprus against contrary winds — the early difficulty, the trials of the voyage beginning.

So the witnesses make the verse the difficult sailing against contrary winds. The lesson the expositors draw is the early difficulty of the voyage, the contrary winds foreshadowing the trials to come. The witnesses note the course: "we sailed under Cyprus, because the winds were contrary" — Barnes notes "sailing under Cyprus" means they sailed along its coast, keeping near it; instead of the direct course in the open sea, they kept the island between them and the westerly winds, breaking the force of the wind; the difficult navigation. The expositors observe the contrary winds: "because the winds were contrary" — the winds opposed the voyage, forcing the ship to seek the shelter of the island; the early difficulty; the contrary winds hindering. The witnesses press the trials beginning: the contrary winds were the beginning of the difficulties that would mount to the great storm (v.14); the early trials; the voyage hard from the start; the troubles beginning. The expositors draw the lesson on the trials of the believer's course: the voyage, beset by contrary winds, pictures the believer's course beset by trials; the contrary winds of opposition; the difficult passage; "we must through much tribulation enter into the kingdom" (Acts 14:22); the hard voyage. The witnesses observe the providence even in the contrary winds: though the winds were contrary, the providence still carried Paul toward Rome; the providence working through the difficulties; the contrary winds not defeating the purpose; the will of God accomplished despite the hindrances. The expositors note the prudent seamanship: the sailors prudently kept under the lee of Cyprus to break the wind; the use of skill and means; the prudence in the difficulty; the seamanship employed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the contrary winds, the difficult voyage, and the trials beginning. The verse thus records the ship sailing under Cyprus against contrary winds — teaching the early difficulty of the voyage, the contrary winds foreshadowing the trials to come.
5:
"And when we had sailed over the sea of Cilicia and Pamphylia, we came to Myra, a city of Lycia." The witnesses note the continued voyage to Myra. Gill notes the seas of Cilicia and Pamphylia joined, and that Myra was a city of Lycia. Barnes notes the sea off the coast of these provinces. The witnesses note the ship sailing across the seas of Cilicia and Pamphylia and arriving at Myra in Lycia — the stage of the voyage where they would change ships; the journey continued through difficulty, the providence still ordering the course toward Rome. The witnesses note the voyage to Myra. The witnesses note the continued course.

The witnesses note the voyage to Myra — the journey continued, the providence ordering the course toward Rome.

So the witnesses make the verse the continued voyage to Myra. The lesson the expositors draw is the journey continued through difficulty, the providence ordering the course. The witnesses note the seas crossed: "we had sailed over the sea of Cilicia and Pamphylia" — the ship crossed the seas off Cilicia and Pamphylia; Gill notes these seas joined; the difficult passage; the voyage proceeding along the coast. The expositors observe the arrival at Myra: "we came to Myra, a city of Lycia" — the ship arrived at Myra in Lycia; the stage of the voyage; the place where they would change ships (v.6); the journey continued. The witnesses press the continued course: through the difficult seas, against the contrary winds, the ship proceeded to Myra; the journey continued through difficulty; the voyage advancing; the course maintained. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence ordering the course: through the difficult passage, the providence ordered the course toward Rome; the journey directed by God; the providence in the route; the apostle borne onward. The witnesses observe the change of ships to come: at Myra the centurion would find another ship for Italy (v.6); the providence arranging the means; the journey continued by another vessel; the course ordered through the change. The expositors note the perseverance of the voyage: despite the difficulties, the voyage persevered to Myra; the steady progress through trial; the journey not halted by the contrary winds; the course held. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the voyage to Myra, the continued course, and the providence ordering the journey. The verse thus records the continued voyage to Myra — teaching the journey continued through difficulty, the providence ordering the course toward Rome.
6:
"And there the centurion found a ship of Alexandria sailing into Italy; and he put us therein." The witnesses note the change to the Alexandrian ship. Gill notes Alexandria was the chief city of Egypt; from its famous port ships were sent for trade, and one was found at Myra bound for Italy. The witnesses note the centurion finding an Alexandrian ship (a grain ship bound for Italy) and putting Paul's company aboard — the providence arranging the means of the journey; the great corn ship that would carry them into the storm and to the shipwreck, yet toward the appointed Rome. The witnesses note the Alexandrian ship. The witnesses note the providence arranging the means.

The witnesses note the change to the Alexandrian grain ship — the providence arranging the means of the journey to Rome.

So the witnesses make the verse the change to the Alexandrian ship. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence arranging the means of the journey, even the ship that would carry them into the storm. The witnesses note the ship: "the centurion found a ship of Alexandria sailing into Italy" — the centurion found an Alexandrian ship bound for Italy; Gill notes Alexandria was the chief city of Egypt, its port famous for trade; the great corn ship (the grain trade from Egypt to Rome); the vessel for the next stage. The expositors observe the transfer: "he put us therein" — the centurion put Paul's company aboard the Alexandrian ship; the change of vessel; the journey continued by the new ship; the transfer arranged. The witnesses press the providence in the means: the providence arranged the ship that would carry them to Rome — the very ship that would meet the storm and be wrecked, yet bring them (through the wreck) toward the appointed Rome; the providence in the means; the vessel ordained; the journey directed. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence ordering the means: God ordered the very ship, the very voyage, that would bring Paul to Rome — even through storm and shipwreck; the providence in the smallest arrangements; the means ordained; "a man's heart deviseth his way: but the LORD directeth his steps" (Proverbs 16:9); the providence directing. The witnesses observe the ship that would be wrecked: this Alexandrian ship would meet the great storm (v.14) and be wrecked (v.41); yet on it Paul and all would be preserved (v.44); the providence using even the doomed ship to carry them toward deliverance; the means that would fail, yet serve the purpose. The expositors note the grain ship's significance: the Alexandrian grain ships were large, carrying corn from Egypt to Rome; the great vessel, with 276 aboard (v.37); the providence in the large ship; the means proportioned to the deliverance to come. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Alexandrian ship, the providence arranging the means, and the vessel ordained for the journey. The verse thus records the change to the Alexandrian ship — teaching the providence arranging the means of the journey, even the ship that would carry them into the storm and toward Rome.
7:
"And when we had sailed slowly many days, and scarce were come over against Cnidus, the wind not suffering us, we sailed under Crete, over against Salmone;" The witnesses note the slow, difficult sailing. Barnes notes they sailed slowly by reason of the prevalence of the western winds; scarcely able to come over against Cnidus (a city on a promontory in Asia Minor); the wind not suffering them to sail directly, so they sailed under (the lee of) Crete. Gill notes the contrary winds. The witnesses note the ship sailing slowly for many days against the wind, scarcely reaching Cnidus, then forced under the lee of Crete — the persistent difficulty, the contrary winds hindering and delaying; the trials mounting. The witnesses note the slow sailing. The witnesses note the persistent difficulty.

The witnesses note the slow, difficult sailing against the wind — the persistent difficulty, the trials mounting toward the storm.

So the witnesses make the verse the slow, difficult sailing. The lesson the expositors draw is the persistent difficulty of the voyage, the contrary winds hindering and delaying. The witnesses note the slowness: "when we had sailed slowly many days, and scarce were come over against Cnidus" — Barnes notes they sailed slowly by reason of the prevailing western winds, scarcely able to reach Cnidus; the slow, laborious progress; the difficult sailing. The expositors observe the wind's hindrance: "the wind not suffering us, we sailed under Crete, over against Salmone" — the wind would not suffer them to sail directly, so they sailed under the lee of Crete; the wind hindering the course; the ship forced from its intended route; the contrary wind driving them. The witnesses press the persistent difficulty: the slow sailing, the scarcely-reached Cnidus, the forced course under Crete — the persistent difficulty; the contrary winds hindering and delaying; the trials mounting; the hard voyage. The expositors draw the lesson on the mounting trials: the difficulties mounted — the slow progress, the contrary winds, the forced course; the trials increasing toward the great storm (v.14); the troubles compounding; the voyage harder; the trial deepening. The witnesses observe the delay that would prove costly: the slow sailing consumed the time, so that the safe season for navigation passed (v.9); the delay bringing them into the dangerous season; the lost time; the difficulty leading toward the storm. The expositors note the perseverance amid difficulty: despite the slow, hard sailing, the voyage persevered; the steady effort against the wind; the seamanship persisting; the course pursued. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the slow sailing, the persistent difficulty, and the trials mounting. The verse thus records the slow, difficult sailing — against the wind, under Crete — teaching the persistent difficulty of the voyage, the contrary winds hindering and delaying toward the storm.
8:
"And, hardly passing it, came unto a place which is called The fair havens; nigh whereunto was the city of Lasea." The witnesses note the difficult arrival at the Fair Havens. Barnes notes they scarcely passed Salmone without being wrecked, almost driven on it; they passed round the east end of Crete, being unable to sail directly; the Fair Havens, on the south-eastern part of Crete, was probably an open road affording good anchorage for a time. The witnesses note the ship, with difficulty, reaching the Fair Havens near Lasea — the place of refuge gained with hardship; the temporary shelter where the fateful decision to sail on would be made; the difficult passage to a precarious rest. The witnesses note the Fair Havens. The witnesses note the difficult arrival.

The witnesses note the difficult arrival at the Fair Havens — the temporary shelter where the fateful decision would be made.

So the witnesses make the verse the difficult arrival at the Fair Havens. The lesson the expositors draw is the place of refuge gained with hardship, the temporary shelter where the fateful decision would be made. The witnesses note the difficulty: "hardly passing it, came unto a place which is called The fair havens" — Barnes notes they scarcely passed Salmone without being wrecked, almost driven on it, passing round the east end of Crete; the difficult passage; the hard-won arrival. The expositors observe the Fair Havens: "a place which is called The fair havens; nigh whereunto was the city of Lasea" — the ship reached the Fair Havens (on the south-eastern part of Crete, near Lasea); Barnes notes it was probably an open road affording good anchorage for a time; the temporary shelter; the place of rest. The witnesses press the precariousness of the shelter: the Fair Havens was a fair-weather haven, not commodious to winter in (v.12); the temporary, precarious shelter; the rest insecure; the place where the decision to sail on would prove fateful. The expositors draw the lesson on the place of decision: at the Fair Havens the decision would be made whether to winter there or sail on; the fateful choice; the place of the decision that would lead into the storm; the crossroads of the voyage. The witnesses observe the refuge gained with hardship: the ship reached the haven with difficulty, scarcely passing the dangers; the refuge hard-won; the shelter gained through hardship; the rest after struggle. The expositors note the contrast of the name and the danger: the "Fair Havens" — yet from this fair-seeming place the ship would sail into the storm; the fair name and the coming danger; the precarious fairness; the shelter that would be left for peril. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Fair Havens, the difficult arrival, and the place where the fateful decision would be made. The verse thus records the difficult arrival at the Fair Havens — teaching the place of refuge gained with hardship, the temporary shelter where the fateful decision would be made.
9:
"Now when much time was spent, and when sailing was now dangerous, because the fast was now already past, Paul admonished them," The witnesses note the dangerous season and Paul's admonition. Barnes notes much time had been spent in the difficult sailing; when they started they had hoped to reach Italy before the dangerous season, but had been detained; "the fast" was the Day of Atonement (in late September or October), after which the Mediterranean navigation became perilous. Gill notes the long delay. The witnesses note that, the safe season being past and sailing now dangerous, Paul admonished the company — the apostle's prudent warning, the season of peril come, the man of God counselling caution. The witnesses note the dangerous season. The witnesses note Paul's admonition.

The witnesses note the dangerous season come and Paul's admonition — the man of God counselling caution as peril neared.

So the witnesses make the verse the dangerous season and Paul's admonition. The lesson the expositors draw is the season of peril come, and the prudent warning of the man of God. The witnesses note the time spent: "when much time was spent" — much time had been consumed in the difficult sailing; Barnes notes they had hoped to reach Italy before the dangerous season, but had been detained; the lost time; the delay. The expositors observe the dangerous season: "sailing was now dangerous, because the fast was now already past" — Barnes notes "the fast" was the Day of Atonement (in late September or October), after which Mediterranean navigation became perilous; the season of storms come; the dangerous time; the peril of the sea. The witnesses press the admonition of Paul: "Paul admonished them" — the apostle, seeing the danger, admonished the company; the prudent warning; the man of God counselling caution; the apostle's concern for their safety. The expositors draw the lesson on the prudence of Paul: Paul, whether by his knowledge of navigation (having been often at sea, 2 Corinthians 11:25) or by divine intimation, perceived the danger and warned them; the prudence of the apostle; the wise counsel; the godly man's foresight. The witnesses observe the apostle's place in the counsel: Paul, a prisoner, yet took part in the counsel, warning the company; the man of God respected enough to speak; the apostle's concern for all aboard; the prudent voice. The expositors note the season's significance: the passing of the fast (the Day of Atonement) marked the onset of the dangerous season; the time noted by the Jewish reckoning (Luke's detail); the historical precision; the peril dated. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the dangerous season, Paul's admonition, and the prudent warning of the man of God. The verse thus records the dangerous season and Paul's admonition — teaching the season of peril come and the prudent warning of the man of God.
10:
"And said unto them, Sirs, I perceive that this voyage will be with hurt and much damage, not only of the lading and ship, but also of our lives." The witnesses note Paul's warning of the danger. Gill notes Paul foresaw and foretold the danger by a spirit of prophecy with which he was endued. Barnes notes it is not certain Paul understood this by direct inspiration; he might have perceived it from his own knowledge of the danger of navigation at the autumnal equinox and from what he saw of the ship. The witnesses note Paul warning the company that the voyage would bring hurt and loss, of cargo, ship, and lives — the prudent (or prophetic) warning of the man of God, whether by inspiration or by sound judgment; the counsel that would be ignored. The witnesses note the warning of danger. The witnesses note prophecy or prudence.

The witnesses note Paul warning of hurt and loss — the man of God's counsel, whether by prophecy or prudent judgment.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's warning of the danger. The lesson the expositors draw is the prudent (or prophetic) warning of the man of God, whether by inspiration or by sound judgment. The witnesses note the warning: "I perceive that this voyage will be with hurt and much damage, not only of the lading and ship, but also of our lives" — Paul warns that the voyage would bring hurt and loss — of cargo, ship, and lives; the apostle's warning of the danger; the counsel of caution. The expositors weigh whether by prophecy or prudence: Gill holds Paul foresaw the danger by a spirit of prophecy; Barnes holds it is not certain it was direct inspiration — Paul might have perceived it from his knowledge of the danger of navigation at the equinox and from what he saw of the ship; the warning either prophetic or prudent; the question of the source. The witnesses present both views: some hold Paul spoke by prophetic foresight (the spirit of prophecy); others hold he spoke by sound judgment (his experience of the sea and the season); either way, the warning was wise and true; the prophecy or the prudence. The expositors draw the lesson on the man of God's counsel: whether by inspiration or judgment, Paul gave wise counsel; the godly man's prudence and foresight; the counsel of the wise; the warning to be heeded. The witnesses observe the comprehensiveness of the warning: Paul warned of loss of cargo, ship, and lives; the full danger named; the apostle's concern for all; the warning of the whole peril. The expositors note that the warning of loss of life was not absolute: later (v.22) Paul, by the angel's message, assured them no life would be lost; the first warning of the danger if they sailed, the later assurance of preservation by God's mercy; the warning and the promise; the danger real, the deliverance of grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's warning, the prophecy or prudence, and the counsel of the man of God. The verse thus records Paul's warning of the danger — hurt and loss to come — teaching the prudent or prophetic warning of the man of God, whether by inspiration or sound judgment.
11:
"Nevertheless the centurion believed the master and the owner of the ship, more than those things which were spoken by Paul." The witnesses note the centurion preferring the experts to Paul. Gill notes the master (governor/pilot) sat at the helm and directed the ship. The witnesses note that the centurion, naturally, believed the master and owner of the ship — the experts in seamanship — rather than Paul's warning; the human counsel preferred to the man of God; the error of trusting worldly expertise against the wisdom of the godly; the warning set aside. The witnesses note the experts preferred. The witnesses note the warning set aside.

The witnesses note the centurion preferring the ship's experts to Paul — the human counsel preferred to the man of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the centurion preferring the experts to Paul. The lesson the expositors draw is the error of trusting worldly expertise against the wisdom of the godly. The witnesses note the preference: "the centurion believed the master and the owner of the ship, more than those things which were spoken by Paul" — the centurion believed the ship's master and owner (the experts in seamanship) rather than Paul; the human counsel preferred; the experts trusted. The expositors observe the naturalness of the choice: the centurion, naturally, trusted the seamen in matters of the sea rather than the prisoner; the worldly judgment preferring the worldly experts; the natural preference; the seamen credited over the apostle. The witnesses press the error: the centurion preferred the master and owner to Paul — the worldly experts to the man of God; the error of trusting human expertise against the wisdom of the godly; the warning set aside; the counsel of God despised; the natural man's choice. The expositors draw the lesson on trusting the godly: the event proved Paul right and the experts wrong; the wisdom of the godly above the expertise of the world in this matter; the man of God to be heeded; "the foolishness of God is wiser than men" (1 Corinthians 1:25); the godly counsel vindicated. The witnesses observe the cost of ignoring the warning: by preferring the experts, the centurion led the company into the storm and the loss; the cost of ignoring the man of God; the warning despised bringing the danger; the error costly. The expositors note the mixture of motives: the master and owner had an interest in proceeding (the cargo, the profit, v.12); the experts not disinterested; the worldly counsel biased by gain; the warning of the disinterested apostle set aside for the interested experts. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the experts preferred, the warning set aside, and the error of trusting the world against the godly. The verse thus records the centurion preferring the experts to Paul — teaching the error of trusting worldly expertise against the wisdom of the godly, the warning of the man of God set aside.
12:
"And because the haven was not commodious to winter in, the more part advised to depart thence also, if by any means they might attain to Phenice, and there to winter; which is an haven of Crete, and lieth toward the south west and north west." The witnesses note the majority's counsel to sail on. Gill notes the Fair Havens was not commodious to winter in — a good summer haven, but an open road exposed to the violent sea in stormy weather, unfit for winter. The witnesses note that, the haven being unsuitable for wintering, the majority advised to sail on to Phenice (a better harbour of Crete) to winter there — the counsel of the majority, the human reasoning that preferred the better harbour, leading them out into the storm; the will of the many against the warning. The witnesses note the unsuitable haven. The witnesses note the majority's counsel.

The witnesses note the majority advising to sail on to a better harbour — the counsel of the many leading out into the storm.

So the witnesses make the verse the majority's counsel to sail on. The lesson the expositors draw is the counsel of the majority, the human reasoning that led them out into the storm against the warning. The witnesses note the unsuitable haven: "the haven was not commodious to winter in" — the Fair Havens was not fit for wintering; Gill notes it was a good summer haven, but an open road exposed to the violent sea in stormy weather, unfit for winter; the reason for the counsel; the haven inadequate. The expositors observe the majority's advice: "the more part advised to depart thence also, if by any means they might attain to Phenice, and there to winter" — the majority advised to sail on to Phenice (a better harbour of Crete) to winter; the counsel of the many; the reasoning that preferred the better harbour. The witnesses press the human reasoning: the majority reasoned that the Fair Havens was unfit, and Phenice better; the prudent-seeming counsel; yet it led them out into the storm; the human reasoning against Paul's warning; the will of the many. The expositors draw the lesson on the danger of majority counsel against the godly: the majority preferred their reasoning to Paul's warning; the counsel of the many overruling the man of God; the multitude not always right; the warning set aside by the majority; "thou shalt not follow a multitude to do evil" (Exodus 23:2); the error of the many. The witnesses observe the plausibility of the counsel: the advice to seek a better harbour seemed prudent; the reasoning plausible; yet it led into the storm; the plausible counsel that erred; the seeming wisdom that failed. The expositors note the providence overruling the counsel: though the majority's counsel led into the storm, the providence overruled — for through the storm and wreck, all would be brought safe to land (v.44) and Paul to Rome; the providence using even the mistaken counsel; the error overruled for the purpose. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the unsuitable haven, the majority's counsel, and the human reasoning that led out into the storm. The verse thus records the majority's counsel to sail on — to seek a better harbour — teaching the counsel of the majority that led them out into the storm against the warning of the man of God.
13:
"And when the south wind blew softly, supposing that they had obtained their purpose, loosing thence, they sailed close by Crete." The witnesses note the deceptive favourable wind. Barnes notes the wind, before a headwind from the west, now veered to the south and blew gently; they supposed they could sail safely along the coast of Crete, and so set sail. The witnesses note the gentle south wind tempting them to sail, supposing they had gained their purpose — the deceptive calm before the storm, the favourable appearance that lured them out; the trap of the easy beginning, the false security that preceded the tempest. The witnesses note the deceptive wind. The witnesses note the false security.

The witnesses note the gentle south wind luring them out — the deceptive calm, the false security before the storm.

So the witnesses make the verse the deceptive favourable wind. The lesson the expositors draw is the false security that lured them out, the deceptive calm before the storm. The witnesses note the gentle wind: "when the south wind blew softly" — Barnes notes the wind, before a headwind from the west, now veered to the south and blew gently; the favourable-seeming wind; the gentle breeze; the appearance of fair sailing. The expositors observe the supposition: "supposing that they had obtained their purpose, loosing thence, they sailed close by Crete" — they supposed they could now sail safely to Phenice, and set sail along the coast of Crete; the false confidence; the purpose seemingly gained; the venture begun. The witnesses press the deceptiveness: the gentle south wind was deceptive — it lured them out, but soon gave way to the tempestuous Euroclydon (v.14); the deceptive calm; the favourable appearance that betrayed them; the false security before the storm. The expositors draw the lesson on the danger of false security: the gentle wind gave a false security, and they ventured out, only to meet the storm; the deceptive ease; the false confidence that precedes the trial; "when they shall say, Peace and safety; then sudden destruction cometh" (1 Thessalonians 5:3); the peril of the deceptive calm. The witnesses observe the venturing on appearances: they sailed on the appearance of a favourable wind, against Paul's warning; the venturing on appearances; the trusting the fair seeming; the error of judging by the surface. The expositors note the providence behind the deceptive calm: the gentle wind that lured them out served the providence — for it brought them into the storm that would, through the wreck, fulfil God's purpose (Paul to Rome, all preserved); the providence using even the deceptive wind; the calm and the storm both ordered. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the deceptive wind, the false security, and the venturing on appearances. The verse thus records the deceptive favourable wind — luring them out — teaching the false security that preceded the storm, the deceptive calm before the tempest.
14:
"But not long after there arose against it a tempestuous wind, called Euroclydon." The witnesses note the sudden tempest. Barnes notes the wind beat violently against Crete; "Euroclydon" (occurring nowhere else) most probably denotes a wind not blowing steadily from any quarter, but a hurricane veering about to different quarters; such hurricanes abound in the Mediterranean. The witnesses note the tempestuous wind Euroclydon arising suddenly against the ship — the violent hurricane that turned the deceptive calm into peril; the storm that would drive them helpless for fourteen days; the trial breaking upon them. The witnesses note the tempest. The witnesses note the storm breaking.

The witnesses note the tempestuous Euroclydon arising — the violent hurricane that turned the calm to peril, the trial breaking.

So the witnesses make the verse the sudden tempest. The lesson the expositors draw is the violent storm breaking upon them, the trial that turned the deceptive calm into peril. The witnesses note the suddenness: "not long after there arose against it a tempestuous wind" — not long after they sailed, a tempestuous wind arose; the sudden change; the calm broken; the storm springing up. The expositors observe the nature of the wind: "called Euroclydon" — Barnes notes the word (occurring nowhere else) most probably denotes a wind not blowing steadily from any quarter, but a hurricane veering about; such hurricanes abound in the Mediterranean; the violent, veering tempest; the hurricane. The witnesses press the violence: the tempestuous Euroclydon beat violently against the ship; the storm of great force; the violent hurricane; the peril breaking upon them; the tempest. The expositors draw the lesson on the trial breaking: the deceptive calm (v.13) gave way to the violent storm; the trial breaking upon them; the sudden peril; the calm turned to tempest; "deep calleth unto deep at the noise of thy waterspouts" (Psalm 42:7); the storm of trial. The witnesses observe the sudden reversal: the gentle wind that lured them out was suddenly replaced by the hurricane; the swift reversal; the calm to chaos; the peril sudden; the false security shattered. The expositors note the providence in the storm: the storm, though terrible, was within God's providence — ordered to bring them, through the wreck, to the appointed end (Paul to Rome, all preserved); the providence over the tempest; the storm in God's hand; "stormy wind fulfilling his word" (Psalm 148:8); the tempest ordained. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the tempest, the storm breaking, and the trial that turned calm to peril. The verse thus records the sudden tempest Euroclydon — arising against the ship — teaching the violent storm breaking upon them, the trial that turned the deceptive calm into peril, yet within God's providence.
15:
"And when the ship was caught, and could not bear up into the wind, we let her drive." The witnesses note the ship overpowered by the storm. Barnes notes the ship was caught suddenly by the tempest; could not resist its violence or be directed; seized by the wind and driven so that it became unmanageable; "we let her drive" — they suffered the ship to be borne along by the wind without attempting to control it. The witnesses note the ship caught and overpowered, unable to head into the wind, so they let her drive before the storm — the helplessness of man before the tempest; the ship and all aboard at the mercy of the wind; the extremity of human weakness. The witnesses note the ship overpowered. The witnesses note the helplessness.

The witnesses note the ship overpowered, driven helpless before the storm — the helplessness of man before the tempest.

So the witnesses make the verse the ship overpowered by the storm. The lesson the expositors draw is the helplessness of man before the tempest, the ship and all aboard at the mercy of the wind. The witnesses note the ship caught: "when the ship was caught, and could not bear up into the wind" — Barnes notes the ship was caught suddenly by the tempest, could not resist its violence or be directed, seized by the wind; the ship overpowered; unable to head into the wind; the vessel mastered. The expositors observe the helpless drifting: "we let her drive" — Barnes notes they suffered the ship to be borne along by the wind without attempting to control it; the helpless drifting; the ship unmanageable; the vessel at the mercy of the storm. The witnesses press the helplessness of man: the ship, with all aboard, was overpowered and driven helpless before the storm; the helplessness of man before the tempest; the seamen's skill availing nothing; the vessel at the mercy of the wind; the extremity of human weakness. The expositors draw the lesson on the weakness of man before God's power: the storm, an instrument of God's power, overpowered all the skill and strength of man; the helplessness of man before the elements; the creature's weakness; "they reel to and fro, and stagger like a drunken man, and are at their wit's end" (Psalm 107:27); the extremity of weakness. The witnesses observe the surrender of control: the seamen, unable to control the ship, let her drive; the surrender to the storm; the giving up of control; the helplessness owned; the vessel yielded to the wind. The expositors note the providence guiding the drift: though the ship drove helpless, the providence guided the drift — toward Malta (v.26), where all would be saved; the providence directing even the helpless drift; the ship driven by the wind, yet by God's hand; the course in God's keeping. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ship overpowered, the helplessness, and the providence guiding the drift. The verse thus records the ship overpowered — driven helpless before the storm — teaching the helplessness of man before the tempest, the ship and all aboard at the mercy of the wind, yet in God's keeping.
16:
"And running under a certain island which is called Clauda, we had much work to come by the boat:" The witnesses note the labour to secure the ship's boat. Barnes notes they ran near to a small island (Clauda, south-west of Crete), where the violence of the wind was probably broken; "we had much work to come by the boat" — they had much difficulty securing the small boat (towed behind the ship), which they raised into the ship to prevent its loss. The witnesses note the company, under the brief shelter of Clauda, struggling with much labour to secure the ship's boat — the toil and difficulty of the storm-tossed crew; the hard, anxious labour to preserve the means of escape; the struggle for survival begun. The witnesses note the labour for the boat. The witnesses note the toil of the storm.

The witnesses note the hard labour to secure the boat — the toil and struggle of the storm-tossed crew.

So the witnesses make the verse the labour to secure the boat. The lesson the expositors draw is the toil and difficulty of the storm-tossed crew, the hard labour to preserve the means of escape. The witnesses note the brief shelter: "running under a certain island which is called Clauda" — Barnes notes they ran near a small island (Clauda, south-west of Crete), where the wind's violence was probably broken; the brief shelter; the momentary relief; the lee of the island. The expositors observe the labour: "we had much work to come by the boat" — Barnes notes they had much difficulty securing the small boat (towed behind the ship), raising it into the ship to prevent its loss; the hard work; the difficult task; the toil to secure the boat. The witnesses press the toil of the crew: even under the brief shelter, the crew laboured hard to secure the boat; the toil of the storm; the anxious, difficult labour; the struggle for survival; the hard work amid the tempest. The expositors draw the lesson on the use of means in the storm: the crew, in the storm, used every means to preserve the ship and the boat; the diligent use of means; the labour to survive; the effort employed; the means in the trial. The witnesses observe the boat as the means of escape: the boat, secured with difficulty, was the means of possible escape; the preserving of the means; the small craft a hope; the boat secured. The expositors note the use of the brief respite: the crew used the brief shelter of Clauda to do the necessary work; the wise use of the respite; the labour in the lull; the moment seized. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the labour for the boat, the toil of the storm, and the struggle for survival. The verse thus records the labour to secure the boat — under the shelter of Clauda — teaching the toil and difficulty of the storm-tossed crew, the hard labour to preserve the means of escape.
17:
"Which when they had taken up, they used helps, undergirding the ship; and, fearing lest they should fall into the quicksands, strake sail, and so were driven." The witnesses note the measures to save the ship. Barnes notes that, having secured the boat, they "used helps" — ropes, cables, or chains — undergirding the ship: the ancients passed cables from one side to the other to keep the ship from being destroyed; and, fearing the quicksands (the Syrtis off Africa), they struck sail and were driven. The witnesses note the crew undergirding the ship with cables and, fearing the shoals, lowering the sail and letting her drive — the desperate measures of seamanship to preserve the ship; the diligent use of every means amid the peril. The witnesses note the undergirding. The witnesses note the fear of the quicksands.

The witnesses note the crew undergirding the ship and striking sail — the desperate measures of seamanship to preserve the ship.

So the witnesses make the verse the measures to save the ship. The lesson the expositors draw is the diligent use of every means of seamanship to preserve the ship amid the peril. The witnesses note the undergirding: "they used helps, undergirding the ship" — Barnes notes they used ropes, cables, or chains, undergirding the ship; the ancients passed cables from side to side to keep the ship from being destroyed (frapping); the measure to hold the ship together; the strengthening of the hull. The expositors observe the fear of the quicksands: "fearing lest they should fall into the quicksands, strake sail, and so were driven" — fearing the Syrtis (the dangerous shoals off Africa), they struck (lowered) the sail and let the ship drive; the fear of the shoals; the measure to avoid the sands; the sail lowered. The witnesses press the diligent seamanship: the crew used every means — undergirding the ship, lowering the sail — to preserve the vessel; the diligent seamanship; the desperate measures; the skill employed amid the peril; the effort to survive. The expositors draw the lesson on the use of means amid danger: the crew, in the storm, used all their skill and means to preserve the ship; the diligent use of means; the effort consistent with dependence on God; the means employed; "the horse is prepared against the day of battle: but safety is of the LORD" (Proverbs 21:31); the means and the providence. The witnesses observe the fear that guided the measures: the fear of the quicksands guided the lowering of the sail; the prudent fear; the danger of the shoals avoided; the wise caution; the seamanship guided by knowledge of the peril. The expositors note the helplessness even amid the means: despite all the measures, the ship was still "driven" — at the mercy of the storm; the means availing little; the helplessness amid the effort; the storm prevailing despite the seamanship. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the undergirding, the fear of the quicksands, and the diligent use of means. The verse thus records the measures to save the ship — undergirding and striking sail — teaching the diligent use of every means of seamanship to preserve the ship amid the peril.
18:
"And we being exceedingly tossed with a tempest, the next day they lightened the ship;" The witnesses note the lightening of the ship. Gill notes the condition at sea is described to the life by the Psalmist (Psalm 107) — sometimes lifted up to the heavens, then sinking down to the depths. Barnes notes they lightened the ship by throwing out part of the cargo. The witnesses note the company, violently tossed by the tempest, lightening the ship the next day by casting out cargo — the increasing peril, the desperate jettisoning to save the ship; the storm mounting, the measures growing more desperate; the extremity deepening. The witnesses note the tossing tempest. The witnesses note the lightening of the ship.

The witnesses note the ship violently tossed and lightened — the increasing peril, the desperate jettisoning to save the ship.

So the witnesses make the verse the lightening of the ship. The lesson the expositors draw is the increasing peril, the desperate measures growing as the storm mounted. The witnesses note the violent tossing: "we being exceedingly tossed with a tempest" — the ship was violently tossed by the storm; Gill notes the condition described to the life by the Psalmist (Psalm 107:26) — lifted to the heavens, sinking to the depths; the violent tossing; the peril of the tempest. The expositors observe the lightening: "the next day they lightened the ship" — Barnes notes they lightened the ship by throwing out part of the cargo; the jettisoning; the casting out of goods to lighten the vessel; the desperate measure. The witnesses press the increasing peril: the violent tossing, the lightening of the ship — the peril increasing; the storm mounting; the measures growing more desperate; the extremity deepening; the danger rising. The expositors draw the lesson on the desperate measures of extremity: the crew, in the extremity, cast out the cargo to save the ship; the desperate jettisoning; the sacrifice of goods for survival; the measures of extremity; "they cast forth the wares that were in the ship into the sea, to lighten it" (Jonah 1:5); the casting out in the storm. The witnesses observe the loss of the cargo: the cargo (the valuable goods) was cast out; the loss of property to save life; the goods sacrificed; the cargo abandoned; the loss for survival. The expositors note the fulfilment of Paul's warning: Paul had warned of loss of cargo and ship (v.10); the lightening of the ship began the fulfilment; the warning proved true; the loss Paul foretold; the prophecy confirmed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the tossing tempest, the lightening of the ship, and the increasing peril. The verse thus records the lightening of the ship — casting out cargo — teaching the increasing peril and the desperate measures growing as the storm mounted, fulfilling Paul's warning.
19:
"And the third day we cast out with our own hands the tackling of the ship." The witnesses note the casting out of the tackling. Barnes notes that on the third day they threw over the tackling of the ship — the anchors, sails, cables, baggage, everything not indispensable to its preservation. Clarke notes the supernumerary anchors, cables, and baggage. The witnesses note the company, on the third day of the storm, casting out the ship's tackling with their own hands — the deepening extremity, the sacrifice of the very equipment of the ship; the desperate measures continuing, the peril mounting through the days of the storm. The witnesses note the casting out of tackling. The witnesses note the deepening extremity.

The witnesses note the tackling cast out with their own hands — the deepening extremity, the sacrifice of the ship's equipment.

So the witnesses make the verse the casting out of the tackling. The lesson the expositors draw is the deepening extremity, the sacrifice of the ship's own equipment as the storm continued. The witnesses note the third day: "the third day" — Barnes notes this was the third day of the storm; the storm continuing; the peril prolonged; the days of the tempest. The expositors observe the casting out: "we cast out with our own hands the tackling of the ship" — Barnes notes they threw over the tackling — the anchors, sails, cables, baggage, everything not indispensable; Clarke notes the supernumerary anchors and cables; the sacrifice of the equipment; the jettisoning of the ship's gear. The witnesses press the deepening extremity: after the cargo (v.18), now the very tackling of the ship was cast out; the deepening extremity; the storm wearing them down; the desperate measures continuing; the peril mounting. The expositors draw the lesson on the extremity that strips away all: the storm forced the casting out of cargo, then tackling; all but the bare ship sacrificed; the extremity that strips away all but life itself; the trial that takes all; the stripping of the storm. The witnesses observe the labour "with our own hands": "with our own hands" — even Paul, Luke, and the passengers laboured; all hands engaged; the common labour in the common peril; the toil shared; the desperate effort of all. The expositors note the prolonged trial: three days of storm, and worse to come (the despair of v.20); the prolonged trial; the storm not abating; the peril enduring; the long endurance of the tempest. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the casting out of tackling, the deepening extremity, and the sacrifice of all but life. The verse thus records the casting out of the tackling — with their own hands — teaching the deepening extremity, the sacrifice of the ship's own equipment as the storm continued.
20:
"And when neither sun nor stars in many days appeared, and no small tempest lay on us, all hope that we should be saved was then taken away." The witnesses note the loss of all hope. Gill notes that when neither sun nor stars appeared in the dark, tempestuous days, they had no means to steer or judge their course (before the compass), and were comfortless. The witnesses note that, with neither sun nor stars visible for many days and the tempest unrelenting, all hope of being saved was taken away — the depth of despair, the extremity of human hopelessness; the darkest hour, the very point at which God's deliverance would come; the night before the dawn of grace. The witnesses note the loss of hope. The witnesses note the extremity of despair.

The witnesses note all hope taken away — the depth of despair, the darkest hour before God's deliverance.

So the witnesses make the verse the loss of all hope. The lesson the expositors draw is the extremity of human despair, the darkest hour at which God's deliverance would come. The witnesses note the darkness: "when neither sun nor stars in many days appeared" — Gill notes that with neither sun nor stars visible in the dark, tempestuous days, they had no means to steer or judge their course (before the compass), and were comfortless; the darkness; the loss of all bearings; the comfortless gloom. The expositors observe the unrelenting tempest: "no small tempest lay on us" — the great tempest continued unrelenting; the storm not abating; the peril enduring; the tempest pressing. The witnesses press the loss of all hope: "all hope that we should be saved was then taken away" — all hope of being saved was gone; the depth of despair; the extremity of human hopelessness; the giving up of all; the despair complete. The expositors draw the lesson on the extremity before deliverance: the loss of all hope was the darkest hour — the very point at which God's deliverance (the angel's message, v.23) would come; the extremity before grace; "man's extremity is God's opportunity"; the night before the dawn; the deliverance at the darkest. The witnesses observe the comfortless despair: with no sun, no stars, no abating of the storm, all hope gone; the comfortless despair; the human hopelessness; the depth of the trial; the darkness of the soul. The expositors note the contrast with Paul's coming assurance: into this despair Paul would bring the angel's message of hope (v.22-25); the despair the dark background of the bright promise; the hopelessness that would be met by God's assurance; the contrast of the despair and the deliverance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the loss of hope, the extremity of despair, and the darkest hour before deliverance. The verse thus records the loss of all hope — neither sun nor stars appearing — teaching the extremity of human despair, the darkest hour at which God's deliverance would come.
21:
"But after long abstinence Paul stood forth in the midst of them, and said, Sirs, ye should have hearkened unto me, and not have loosed from Crete, and to have gained this harm and loss." The witnesses note Paul's standing forth with a gentle reproof. Gill notes the long abstinence was from food — not for want of it, but through nausea, fear, and the lack of time amid their labours. Matthew Henry notes the issue of the distress: they escaped with their lives, and that was for Paul's sake. The witnesses note Paul, after the long abstinence, standing forth and gently reminding them they should have heeded his warning — not to upbraid, but to establish his credibility for the good news to follow; the man of God vindicated, about to bring hope. The witnesses note the gentle reproof. The witnesses note the credibility established.

The witnesses note Paul's gentle reproof — reminding them of his warning, not to upbraid, but to establish credibility for the good news.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's standing forth with a gentle reproof. The lesson the expositors draw is the establishing of Paul's credibility, the reminder of his warning serving the good news to follow. The witnesses note the long abstinence: "after long abstinence" — Gill notes the long abstinence from food, not for want of it, but through nausea, fear, and the lack of time amid their labours; the company worn, fasting; the extremity of the distress. The expositors observe Paul standing forth: "Paul stood forth in the midst of them" — Paul, amid the despair, stood forth to speak; the man of God rising in the crisis; the apostle taking the lead; the voice of hope amid despair. The witnesses press the gentle reproof: "ye should have hearkened unto me, and not have loosed from Crete, and to have gained this harm and loss" — Paul reminds them they should have heeded his warning (v.10); the gentle reproof; not to upbraid for its own sake, but to establish his credibility; the reminder serving a purpose. The expositors draw the lesson on the purpose of the reminder: Paul reminds them of his warning not merely to reproach, but to establish his credibility — that they might now believe the good news he was about to bring (v.22); the credibility established; the reminder serving the hope; the warning vindicated to ground the promise. The witnesses observe the vindication of the man of God: the event proved Paul right (the harm and loss had come, as he warned); the man of God vindicated; the warning fulfilled; the apostle's wisdom confirmed; the godly counsel proved true. The expositors note Matthew Henry's observation: the issue of the distress was that they escaped with their lives, and that for Paul's sake; the deliverance for the sake of the godly man; the company spared because of Paul; the value of the one righteous man (v.24). The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the gentle reproof, the credibility established, and the man of God vindicated. The verse thus records Paul's standing forth with a gentle reproof — reminding them of his warning — teaching the establishing of his credibility, the reminder serving the good news to follow.
22:
"And now I exhort you to be of good cheer: for there shall be no loss of any man's life among you, but of the ship." The witnesses note Paul's exhortation and the promise of preservation. Barnes notes this must have been cheering news to those who had given up all for lost; as Paul had shown wisdom in his former counsel, they might now be more disposed to listen; he states the reason he believed they would be safe. The witnesses note Paul exhorting the despairing company to be of good cheer, assuring them that no life would be lost, though the ship would be — the good news brought into the despair; the man of God turning hopelessness into hope by the word of God's promise. The witnesses note the exhortation to good cheer. The witnesses note the promise of preservation.

The witnesses note Paul's exhortation to good cheer and the promise that no life would be lost — hope brought into despair.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's exhortation and the promise of preservation. The lesson the expositors draw is the good news brought into the despair, the man of God turning hopelessness into hope by God's promise. The witnesses note the exhortation: "I exhort you to be of good cheer" — Paul exhorts the despairing company to take courage; the call to good cheer; the encouragement amid despair; the voice of hope. The expositors observe the timeliness of the news: Barnes notes this must have been cheering news to those who had given up all for lost; as Paul had shown wisdom before, they might now listen; the good news in the extremity; the hope at the darkest hour. The witnesses press the promise of preservation: "there shall be no loss of any man's life among you, but of the ship" — Paul assures them no life would be lost, though the ship would be; the promise of preservation; the lives spared; the ship sacrificed but all saved; the gracious assurance. The expositors draw the lesson on the man of God bringing hope: Paul, by the word of God's promise (the angel's message, v.23), turned the despair into hope; the godly man the bearer of hope; the word of God against the despair; the hope grounded in God's promise; the comfort of the promise. The witnesses observe the certainty of the assurance: Paul speaks not a mere hope but a certainty — "there shall be no loss of any man's life"; the confident assurance; the promise sure; the word of God certain; the assurance firm. The expositors note the ground of the assurance (the angel's message, v.23): Paul's assurance rested not on his own judgment, but on the angel's message from God; the promise grounded in revelation; the assurance from God; the hope sure because divine. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the exhortation to good cheer, the promise of preservation, and the hope brought into despair. The verse thus records Paul's exhortation and promise — no loss of life — teaching the good news brought into the despair, the man of God turning hopelessness into hope by God's promise.
23:
"For there stood by me this night the angel of God, whose I am, and whom I serve," The witnesses note the angel's appearance and Paul's confession of his relationship to God. Gill notes the angel, a ministering spirit, was sent by God and appeared to Paul, likely while he was praying for deliverance. Barnes notes God's messages were often communicated by angels (Hebrews 1:14). The witnesses note Paul telling of the angel of God who stood by him, and confessing God as the One "whose I am, and whom I serve" — the ground of the assurance in the angel's message, and the apostle's beautiful confession of belonging to and serving God; the devotion that underlay his confidence. The witnesses note the angel of God. The witnesses note "whose I am, and whom I serve."

The witnesses note the angel's message and Paul's confession — "whose I am, and whom I serve" — the devotion underlying his confidence.

So the witnesses make the verse the angel's appearance and Paul's confession. The lesson the expositors draw is the ground of the assurance in the angel's message and the apostle's beautiful confession of belonging to and serving God. The witnesses note the angel: "there stood by me this night the angel of God" — Gill notes the angel, a ministering spirit, was sent by God and appeared to Paul (likely while he was praying for deliverance); Barnes notes God's messages were often by angels (Hebrews 1:14); the angelic messenger; the divine communication. The expositors observe the ground of the assurance: the assurance Paul gave (v.22) rested on the angel's message from God; the promise grounded in the divine revelation; the angel the bearer of God's word; the assurance from God. The witnesses press the beautiful confession: "whose I am, and whom I serve" — Paul confesses God as the One to whom he belongs and whom he serves; the twofold confession — belonging and serving; the devotion of the apostle; the relationship to God owned. The expositors draw the lesson on belonging to God: "whose I am" — Paul owns that he belongs to God, bought, claimed, possessed; "ye are not your own... ye are bought with a price" (1 Corinthians 6:19-20); the believer God's possession; the belonging confessed. The witnesses observe the service: "and whom I serve" — Paul serves the God to whom he belongs; the belonging issuing in service; the devotion of the servant; the life given to God's service; "whom I serve with my spirit in the gospel" (Romans 1:9); the service flowing from the belonging. The expositors note the comfort of the relationship: because Paul belonged to and served God, God cared for him, sent the angel, gave the promise; the comfort of belonging to God; the care of the Master for his own; the relationship the ground of the confidence. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the angel of God, the confession "whose I am, and whom I serve," and the devotion underlying Paul's confidence. The verse thus records the angel's appearance and Paul's confession — whose I am, and whom I serve — teaching the ground of the assurance in the angel's message and the apostle's devotion to the God who claimed and was served by him.
24:
"Saying, Fear not, Paul; thou must be brought before Caesar: and, lo, God hath given thee all them that sail with thee." The witnesses note the angel's message of preservation. Gill notes that though Paul knew he should go to Rome, the flesh was weak, and he might have fears, which sometimes attend the best of men. Barnes notes "Fear not, Paul" — do not be alarmed at the danger; thou must be brought before Caesar. The witnesses note the angel assuring Paul, "Fear not... thou must be brought before Caesar," and granting him the lives of all who sailed with him — the certainty of God's purpose for Paul, and the gracious gift of the whole company spared for the apostle's sake; the godly man a blessing to all around him. The witnesses note "Fear not, Paul." The witnesses note all given for Paul's sake.

The witnesses note the angel's "Fear not... God hath given thee all them that sail with thee" — the company spared for Paul's sake.

So the witnesses make the verse the angel's message of preservation. The lesson the expositors draw is the certainty of God's purpose for Paul and the gracious gift of the whole company spared for the apostle's sake. The witnesses note the comfort: "Fear not, Paul" — the angel comforts Paul; Gill notes that though Paul knew he should go to Rome, the flesh was weak and he might have fears, which attend the best of men; the comfort to the fearful; the "fear not" of God. The expositors observe the certainty of the purpose: "thou must be brought before Caesar" — the angel assures Paul he must reach Rome and stand before Caesar; the certainty of God's purpose (Acts 23:11); the divine "must"; the appointed end sure; the purpose that the storm could not defeat. The witnesses press the gift of the company: "lo, God hath given thee all them that sail with thee" — God granted Paul the lives of all who sailed with him; the whole company spared for Paul's sake; the gift of the 276 lives; the gracious grant. The expositors draw the lesson on the godly man a blessing to all around: God spared the whole company for Paul's sake; the godly man a blessing to the ungodly around him; the many preserved for the one righteous; "the LORD blessed the Egyptian's house for Joseph's sake" (Genesis 39:5); the value of the godly; the blessing through the one. The witnesses observe the answer to Paul's prayer: God "hath given" Paul the company — likely in answer to his prayer for their deliverance; the gift in answer to prayer; the apostle's intercession heard; the lives granted to his prayer. The expositors note the certainty grounding the assurance: because God's purpose (Paul to Caesar) was certain, and the company given to Paul, all would be preserved; the certainty grounding the promise; the assurance sure because divine; the purpose and the gift securing all. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the "Fear not," the certain purpose, and the company spared for Paul's sake. The verse thus records the angel's message — Fear not... God hath given thee all — teaching the certainty of God's purpose for Paul and the gracious gift of the whole company spared for the apostle's sake.
25:
"Wherefore, sirs, be of good cheer: for I believe God, that it shall be even as it was told me." The witnesses note Paul's faith taking God at his word. Robertson notes "for I believe God" is Paul's reason for his own good cheer and for his exhortation; God had spoken — that was enough. The witnesses note Paul, repeating the call to good cheer, grounding it in his faith — "I believe God, that it shall be even as it was told me" — the faith that takes God at his word, resting the whole confidence on the bare promise of God; the example of faith, believing God against all appearances; the assurance of faith. The witnesses note "I believe God." The witnesses note faith taking God at his word.

The witnesses note Paul's "I believe God" — the faith that takes God at his word, resting all on the bare promise.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's faith taking God at his word. The lesson the expositors draw is the faith that rests the whole confidence on the bare promise of God, believing against all appearances. The witnesses note the renewed exhortation: "be of good cheer" — Paul repeats the call to good cheer (v.22); the encouragement renewed; the call to courage; the confidence pressed. The expositors observe the ground of the cheer: "for I believe God, that it shall be even as it was told me" — Robertson notes this is Paul's reason for his own good cheer and his exhortation; God had spoken — that was enough; the faith grounding the cheer; the confidence in God's word. The witnesses press the faith that takes God at his word: "I believe God" — Paul believes God's promise simply, fully; he takes God at his word; the faith resting on the bare promise; the confidence in the word of God; "he staggered not at the promise of God through unbelief" (Romans 4:20). The expositors draw the lesson on the assurance of faith: Paul believed it would be "even as it was told me" — the faith that holds the promise certain, against all the appearances of the storm; the assurance of faith; the confidence that the word of God will stand; the faith that does not waver; "I know whom I have believed" (2 Timothy 1:12). The witnesses observe the faith against appearances: amid the raging storm, with all hope humanly gone (v.20), Paul believed God; the faith against the appearances; the confidence in the promise despite the tempest; the faith that sees beyond the storm to the word of God. The expositors note the example of faith: Paul's "I believe God" is the example of faith — taking God at his word, resting all on his promise, confident against all appearances; the pattern of faith; the believer to believe God; the example of the trusting heart. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's "I believe God," the faith taking God at his word, and the assurance against all appearances. The verse thus records Paul's faith — I believe God, that it shall be even as it was told me — teaching the faith that rests the whole confidence on the bare promise of God, believing against all appearances.
26:
"Howbeit we must be cast upon a certain island." The witnesses note the prediction of the shipwreck on an island. Gill notes this circumstance was foretold so that, when it came to pass, it might be manifest it was not a fortuitous event of chance, but predetermined by God, made known to the apostle, and predicted by him; the island was Melita (Malta). The witnesses note Paul foretelling that they must be cast upon a certain island — the precise prediction that would mark the deliverance as God's, not chance; the providence both saving them and proving itself by the fulfilled word; the shipwreck foreseen, the island appointed. The witnesses note the predicted island. The witnesses note the providence proved by the word.

The witnesses note the prediction of the island — marking the deliverance as God's, not chance, by the fulfilled word.

So the witnesses make the verse the prediction of the shipwreck on an island. The lesson the expositors draw is the precise prediction that would mark the deliverance as God's, not chance. The witnesses note the prediction: "we must be cast upon a certain island" — Paul foretells that they must be cast upon a certain island; the precise prediction; the shipwreck foreseen; the island appointed. The expositors observe Gill's note on the purpose: this circumstance was foretold so that, when it came to pass, it might be manifest it was not a fortuitous event of chance, but predetermined by God, made known to Paul, and predicted by him; the island was Melita (Malta); the prediction proving the providence. The witnesses press the providence proved by the word: the precise fulfilment of the prediction (the casting upon Malta, Acts 28:1) would prove the deliverance was God's doing, not chance; the providence proved by the fulfilled word; the prediction marking the divine hand; the word confirmed by the event. The expositors draw the lesson on the certainty of God's word: Paul foretold the very manner of the deliverance — cast upon an island; the certainty of God's word; the precise prediction fulfilled; the word of God sure in its details; "the LORD will perform... the thought of his heart" (Psalm 33:11); the word certain. The witnesses observe the "must" of the providence: "we must be cast upon a certain island" — the divine "must"; the appointed manner of the deliverance; the providence ordering even the shipwreck; the necessity of God's plan; the appointed island. The expositors note the deliverance through the shipwreck: the deliverance would come not by saving the ship, but through the shipwreck — cast upon the island; the salvation through the loss; the deliverance by an unexpected way; the providence saving through the wreck. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the predicted island, the providence proved by the word, and the deliverance through the shipwreck. The verse thus records the prediction of the island — we must be cast upon a certain island — teaching the precise prediction that would mark the deliverance as God's, not chance, proved by the fulfilled word.
27:
"But when the fourteenth night was come, as we were driven up and down in Adria, about midnight the shipmen deemed that they drew near to some country;" The witnesses note the approach to land on the fourteenth night. Barnes notes the fourteenth night from the commencement of the tempest; "in Adria" — the Adriatic Sea, the name given by the ancients not only to the gulf but to the whole sea between Greece, Italy, and Africa; the sailors sensed they neared land. The witnesses note the company, driven up and down for fourteen nights in the Adriatic, at midnight the sailors sensing the nearness of land — the long endurance of the storm, the providence guiding the drift toward the appointed deliverance; the dawn of hope after the long night of peril. The witnesses note the fourteenth night. The witnesses note the nearness of land.

The witnesses note the fourteenth night and the sensing of land — the long endurance, the providence guiding toward deliverance.

So the witnesses make the verse the approach to land on the fourteenth night. The lesson the expositors draw is the long endurance of the storm and the providence guiding the drift toward the appointed deliverance. The witnesses note the long endurance: "when the fourteenth night was come, as we were driven up and down in Adria" — Barnes notes the fourteenth night from the commencement of the tempest; the company driven up and down in the Adriatic (Barnes notes the ancient name for the whole sea between Greece, Italy, and Africa); the long endurance; the fourteen nights of storm. The expositors observe the sensing of land: "about midnight the shipmen deemed that they drew near to some country" — at midnight the sailors sensed they neared land (by the sound of breakers, or the change of the sea); the nearness of land; the approach to the shore; the dawn of hope.
