The witnesses press the long endurance of the storm: fourteen nights driven helpless in the storm; the long endurance; the prolonged trial; the weary days and nights; the patience of the company tried. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence guiding the drift: through the fourteen nights, the providence guided the helpless drift toward the appointed island (Malta, v.26); the providence directing the course; the ship driven by the wind, yet by God's hand, to the appointed place; the drift in God's keeping. The witnesses observe the approach to deliverance: the sensing of land was the approach to the deliverance God had promised (v.24-26); the nearness of the shore; the deliverance drawing near; the long night ending; the hope at hand. The expositors note the providence in the timing: after the full appointed time (fourteen nights), the land drew near; the providence in the timing; the trial of the appointed length; the deliverance at the set time; the timing in God's hand. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the fourteenth night, the nearness of land, and the providence guiding toward deliverance. The verse thus records the approach to land on the fourteenth night — the sailors sensing the shore — teaching the long endurance of the storm and the providence guiding the drift toward the appointed deliverance.
28:
"And sounded, and found it twenty fathoms: and when they had gone a little further, they sounded again, and found it fifteen fathoms." The witnesses note the soundings showing the shoaling water. Gill notes the sounding line was a line with a lead at the end, let down into the water to find the depth, by which they could judge whether they were near land. The witnesses note the sailors sounding and finding the water shoaling from twenty to fifteen fathoms — the careful measurement that confirmed the nearness of land; the prudent seamanship watching the depth; the approach to the shore tracked by the lead; the diligence amid the peril. The witnesses note the soundings. The witnesses note the shoaling water.

The witnesses note the soundings showing the shoaling water — the prudent seamanship tracking the approach to land.

So the witnesses make the verse the soundings showing the shoaling water. The lesson the expositors draw is the prudent seamanship tracking the approach to land, the diligence amid the peril. The witnesses note the sounding: "sounded, and found it twenty fathoms" — Gill notes the sounding line was a line with a lead, let down to find the depth, by which they judged their nearness to land; the careful measurement; the lead let down; the depth taken. The expositors observe the shoaling: "when they had gone a little further, they sounded again, and found it fifteen fathoms" — the second sounding showed the water shoaling from twenty to fifteen fathoms; the depth decreasing; the land nearing; the shoaling water. The witnesses press the confirmation of the nearness of land: the shoaling from twenty to fifteen fathoms confirmed they were approaching the shore; the nearness of land confirmed; the approach tracked; the shore drawing near. The expositors draw the lesson on the prudent seamanship: the sailors prudently sounded to track the depth and the approach to land; the diligent seamanship; the careful watching; the prudence amid the peril; the means employed. The witnesses observe the diligence amid the peril: even at midnight, in the storm, the sailors diligently sounded; the diligence in the danger; the careful work; the watchfulness; the prudence maintained. The expositors note the danger of the nearing shore: the shoaling water, while confirming the nearness of land, also brought the danger of running aground on rocks (v.29); the nearness of land both hope and peril; the shore a danger as well as a deliverance; the rocks feared. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the soundings, the shoaling water, and the prudent seamanship tracking the approach. The verse thus records the soundings showing the shoaling water — twenty fathoms, then fifteen — teaching the prudent seamanship tracking the approach to land, the diligence amid the peril.
29:
"Then fearing lest we should have fallen upon rocks, they cast four anchors out of the stern, and wished for the day." The witnesses note the casting of anchors and the longing for day. Gill notes they feared falling upon rocks or shelves as the water shallowed so fast. Barnes notes they cast four anchors to make the ship secure against the violence of the storm, and wished for the day to discern their situation and danger. The witnesses note the sailors, fearing the rocks, casting four anchors from the stern and longing for daylight — the prudent measures and the anxious waiting; the night of suspense, the longing for the light to reveal their hope of escape; the patient endurance till the dawn. The witnesses note the four anchors. The witnesses note the longing for day.

The witnesses note the four anchors cast and the longing for day — the prudent measures and the anxious waiting for the dawn.

So the witnesses make the verse the casting of anchors and the longing for day. The lesson the expositors draw is the prudent measures and the anxious waiting for the dawn of deliverance. The witnesses note the fear of the rocks: "fearing lest we should have fallen upon rocks" — Gill notes they feared falling upon rocks or shelves as the water shallowed fast; the fear of the rocks; the danger of the nearing shore; the peril of running aground in the dark. The expositors observe the anchors: "they cast four anchors out of the stern" — Barnes notes they cast four anchors to make the ship secure against the storm; the prudent measure; the holding of the ship; the anchors steadying her till day. The witnesses press the longing for day: "and wished for the day" — Barnes notes they wished for the day to discern their situation and danger; the longing for light; the anxious waiting; the desire for the dawn; "my soul waiteth for the Lord more than they that watch for the morning" (Psalm 130:6). The expositors draw the lesson on the anxious waiting for deliverance: the company waited anxiously through the night for the day that would reveal their hope of escape; the patient endurance; the longing for the light; the waiting for deliverance; the night of suspense. The witnesses observe the prudent measures amid the promise: though Paul had promised their safety (v.22-24), the sailors still cast anchors and watched; the prudent measures alongside the promise; the means and the assurance together; the diligence not slackened by the promise. The expositors note the picture of hope deferred: the longing for day pictures the soul's longing for deliverance, for the dawn after the night of trial; the hope deferred; the waiting for the morning; the longing for the light of deliverance; the patient hope. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the four anchors, the longing for day, and the anxious waiting for deliverance. The verse thus records the casting of anchors and the longing for day — teaching the prudent measures and the anxious waiting for the dawn of deliverance.
30:
"And as the shipmen were about to flee out of the ship, when they had let down the boat into the sea, under colour as though they would have cast anchors out of the foreship," The witnesses note the sailors' attempted flight. Gill notes the shipmen were about to flee to save their lives, concluding the ship was in the utmost danger. Barnes notes they pretended ("under colour") that it was necessary to get into the boat to carry the anchors ahead, but with a real intention to make for the shore, leaving the prisoners. The witnesses note the sailors, under pretence of laying out anchors, attempting to flee in the boat and abandon the ship — the selfish, deceitful attempt to save themselves at the others' peril; the treachery that would have doomed the rest, about to be foiled. The witnesses note the attempted flight. The witnesses note the deceitful pretence.

The witnesses note the sailors' deceitful attempt to flee in the boat — the selfish treachery that would have doomed the rest.

So the witnesses make the verse the sailors' attempted flight. The lesson the expositors draw is the selfish, deceitful attempt to save themselves at the others' peril, about to be foiled. The witnesses note the attempt: "the shipmen were about to flee out of the ship, when they had let down the boat into the sea" — Gill notes the shipmen were about to flee to save their lives, concluding the ship in utmost danger; the attempted escape; the sailors abandoning the ship. The expositors observe the pretence: "under colour as though they would have cast anchors out of the foreship" — Barnes notes they pretended it was necessary to get into the boat to carry the anchors ahead, but with a real intention to make for the shore, leaving the others; the deceitful pretence; the false cover for the flight. The witnesses press the selfishness and treachery: the sailors would save themselves in the boat, abandoning the prisoners and passengers to their fate; the selfish flight; the treachery; the desertion of the helpless; the self-preservation at the others' peril. The expositors draw the lesson on the selfishness of the natural man in danger: in the extremity, the sailors thought only of themselves, deceitfully abandoning the rest; the selfishness of the natural man; the every-man-for-himself of the godless; the treachery in the crisis; the self-love that deserts others. The witnesses observe the danger to the rest: had the sailors fled, the ship (without their skill) would have been lost, and the rest doomed; the treachery that would have undone the deliverance; the flight imperilling all; the desertion dangerous. The expositors note the contrast with Paul's care for all: the sailors sought to save only themselves; Paul sought the salvation of all (v.24, 31); the selfishness against the love; the deserter against the intercessor; the contrast of the natural man and the godly. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the attempted flight, the deceitful pretence, and the selfish treachery. The verse thus records the sailors' attempted flight — under pretence of laying anchors — teaching the selfish, deceitful attempt to save themselves at the others' peril, about to be foiled.
31:
"Paul said to the centurion and to the soldiers, Except these abide in the ship, ye cannot be saved." The witnesses note Paul's insistence on the use of means. Gill notes Paul directed his speech to the centurion and soldiers (not the master and owner, who may have been in the scheme), who had no knowledge of maritime affairs and depended on Paul's assurance. The witnesses note Paul declaring that unless the sailors stayed in the ship, the rest could not be saved — the harmony of the certain promise and the necessary means; though God had promised all would be saved (v.24), the means (the sailors' skill) must be used; the providence and the means joined, not opposed. The witnesses note the use of means. The witnesses note the means and the promise.

The witnesses note Paul insisting the sailors stay — the certain promise and the necessary means joined, not opposed.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's insistence on the use of means. The lesson the expositors draw is the harmony of the certain promise and the necessary means, the providence and the means joined. The witnesses note the address: "Paul said to the centurion and to the soldiers" — Gill notes Paul addressed the centurion and soldiers (not the master and owner, who may have been in the scheme), who depended on his assurance; the warning to those who would act; the timely word. The expositors observe the insistence on the means: "Except these abide in the ship, ye cannot be saved" — Paul declares that unless the sailors (with their skill) stayed in the ship, the rest could not be saved; the necessity of the means; the sailors' skill needed; the means insisted on. The witnesses press the harmony of the promise and the means: though God had promised that all would be saved (v.24), Paul insists the means (the sailors' skill) must be used; the certain promise and the necessary means joined, not opposed; the providence working through means; the end and the means together. The expositors draw the lesson on providence and means: God's certain decree does not exclude the use of means, but includes them; the promise of preservation was to be fulfilled through the means (the sailors staying); the providence and the means in harmony; "the means are as much appointed as the end"; the use of means consistent with the certainty of the promise; the Reformed harmony. The witnesses observe the answer to fatalism: Paul does not say, "God has promised, so we need do nothing"; rather, "the means must be used"; the certain promise not breeding idleness, but requiring the means; the answer to fatalism; the faith that uses means. The expositors note the wisdom and watchfulness of Paul: Paul, watchful, perceived the sailors' design and acted to prevent it; the apostle's wisdom; the watchfulness that saved all; the prudence in the crisis; the man of God alert. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the use of means, the harmony of the promise and the means, and the answer to fatalism. The verse thus records Paul's insistence on the means — except these abide in the ship, ye cannot be saved — teaching the harmony of the certain promise and the necessary means, the providence and the means joined, not opposed.
32:
"Then the soldiers cut off the ropes of the boat, and let her fall off." The witnesses note the soldiers' decisive action. Barnes notes the mariners had not yet got into the boat; by cutting the ropes and letting the boat fall away, the soldiers removed all possibility of the sailors' fleeing, compelling them to remain and use their skill for the common safety. The witnesses note the soldiers, trusting Paul, cutting the boat loose so the sailors could not flee — the decisive action that secured the means of deliverance; the prompt obedience to the man of God's word, foiling the treachery and binding all to the common effort. The witnesses note the boat cut loose. The witnesses note the decisive action.

The witnesses note the soldiers cutting the boat loose — the decisive action securing the means, foiling the sailors' flight.

So the witnesses make the verse the soldiers' decisive action. The lesson the expositors draw is the decisive action that secured the means of deliverance, foiling the treachery. The witnesses note the cutting loose: "the soldiers cut off the ropes of the boat, and let her fall off" — Barnes notes the mariners had not yet got into the boat; by cutting the ropes and letting it fall away, the soldiers removed all possibility of the sailors' fleeing; the boat cast off; the means of escape removed. The expositors observe the effect: the soldiers, by cutting the boat loose, compelled the sailors to remain and use their skill for the common safety; the sailors bound to the ship; the means secured; the treachery foiled. The witnesses press the prompt obedience to Paul's word: the soldiers, trusting Paul's word (v.31), acted at once to cut the boat loose; the prompt obedience; the man of God's word heeded; the decisive action on the apostle's counsel; the trust acted on. The expositors draw the lesson on the decisive action securing the means: the soldiers' decisive action secured the means of deliverance — the sailors' skill — for all; the prompt securing of the means; the deliverance safeguarded; the decisive obedience; the means preserved. The witnesses observe the foiling of the treachery: the sailors' deceitful flight (v.30) was foiled by the soldiers' action; the treachery undone; the selfish plan defeated; the deliverance secured against the desertion; the plot foiled. The expositors note the providence in the soldiers' trust: the soldiers, who had once preferred the experts to Paul (v.11), now trusted his word and acted; the change wrought by the fulfilled warning; the providence inclining them to heed; the man of God now believed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the boat cut loose, the decisive action, and the securing of the means. The verse thus records the soldiers' decisive action — cutting the boat loose — teaching the decisive action that secured the means of deliverance, foiling the sailors' treacherous flight.
33:
"And while the day was coming on, Paul besought them all to take meat, saying, This day is the fourteenth day that ye have tarried and continued fasting, having taken nothing." The witnesses note Paul's call to take food. Barnes notes that at daybreak, before they had light to act, Paul urged them to take food; "having taken nothing" cannot mean they had eaten nothing at all, but that they had had no regular meal, being too anxious and busy. The witnesses note Paul, as day approached, urging the whole company, worn by fourteen days of fasting and toil, to take food — the apostle's practical, fatherly care for their bodily need; the leader providing for the strength of all before the final effort; the wisdom that tends the body as well as the soul. The witnesses note the call to eat. The witnesses note Paul's fatherly care.

The witnesses note Paul urging the worn company to eat — the apostle's practical, fatherly care for their bodily need.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's call to take food. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostle's practical, fatherly care for the bodily need of the company. The witnesses note the timing: "while the day was coming on, Paul besought them all to take meat" — Barnes notes that at daybreak, before they had light to act, Paul urged them to take food; the timely call; the apostle providing before the effort; the food before the labour. The expositors observe the long fasting: "this day is the fourteenth day that ye have tarried and continued fasting, having taken nothing" — Barnes notes "having taken nothing" cannot mean they had eaten nothing at all, but that they had had no regular meal, being too anxious and busy; the long fasting; the company worn by the fourteen days of toil and fear; the bodies weakened. The witnesses press Paul's practical care: Paul urged the worn company to take food, to strengthen them for the final effort; the apostle's practical, fatherly care; the leader providing for the bodily need; the care that tends the body. The expositors draw the lesson on the care for the body: Paul, the man of God, cared not only for souls but for bodies; the practical care; the wisdom that tends the body as well as the soul; the religion that does not neglect the body's need; "the body is for the Lord" (1 Corinthians 6:13); the holistic care. The witnesses observe the leadership of Paul: Paul, though a prisoner, had become the leader of the company — counselling, encouraging, and now providing for their strength; the man of God leading; the apostle's authority by grace; the leader in the crisis; the providence making him the head. The expositors note the wisdom of strengthening before the effort: Paul wisely urged them to eat before the final effort of reaching land; the prudence of preparation; the strength for the task; the wise provision; the body fortified for the labour. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the call to eat, Paul's fatherly care, and the wisdom that tends the body. The verse thus records Paul's call to take food — after the fourteen days' fasting — teaching the apostle's practical, fatherly care for the bodily need of the company.
34:
"Wherefore I pray you to take some meat: for this is for your health: for there shall not an hair fall from the head of any of you." The witnesses note Paul's renewed urging and the assurance of safety. Barnes notes "not an hair fall from the head" is a proverbial expression denoting that they should be preserved entirely safe, none lost, experiencing not the least harm (1 Samuel 14:45; 1 Kings 1:52; Matthew 10:30). The witnesses note Paul urging them to eat for their health and strength, and renewing the assurance that not a hair of any of them would perish — the practical counsel joined to the confident promise; the apostle providing for the body while assuring them of God's complete preservation. The witnesses note "for your health." The witnesses note "not an hair shall fall."

The witnesses note Paul urging food for health and assuring "not a hair shall fall" — practical counsel joined to the confident promise.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's renewed urging and assurance. The lesson the expositors draw is the practical counsel joined to the confident promise of God's complete preservation. The witnesses note the urging: "I pray you to take some meat: for this is for your health" — Paul urges them to eat for their health and strength; the practical counsel; the food for the body's good; the care for their strength. The expositors observe the assurance: "there shall not an hair fall from the head of any of you" — Barnes notes this is a proverbial expression denoting that they should be preserved entirely safe, none lost, experiencing not the least harm (1 Samuel 14:45; 1 Kings 1:52; Matthew 10:30); the complete preservation; the total safety assured; the gracious promise. The witnesses press the practical counsel joined to the promise: Paul joins the practical (eat for your health) to the confident promise (not a hair shall fall); the care for the body and the assurance of safety together; the practical and the spiritual joined; the counsel and the comfort. The witnesses observe the confidence renewed: Paul renews the assurance (v.22, 24) that all would be preserved; the confidence repeated; the promise pressed; the assurance grounding the courage; the comfort renewed. The expositors note the harmony of the means and the assurance: Paul urges them to eat (the means of strength) even as he assures them of safety (the promise); the means and the assurance joined; the practical care and the confident promise together; the diligence and the faith. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the urging for health, the assurance "not a hair shall fall," and the practical counsel joined to the promise. The verse thus records Paul's renewed urging and assurance — for your health... not a hair shall fall — teaching the practical counsel joined to the confident promise of God's complete preservation.
35:
"And when he had thus spoken, he took bread, and gave thanks to God in presence of them all: and when he had broken it, he began to eat." The witnesses note Paul's giving of thanks and example. Gill notes this was not the Lord's supper, as some suggested, but common bread such as seafaring men eat; the apostle gave thanks before eating. The witnesses note Paul taking bread, giving thanks to God before all, breaking it, and beginning to eat — the example of thanksgiving and calm faith; the apostle openly owning God in the giving of thanks, even amid the storm, and leading the company by his own example to take courage and eat. The witnesses note the giving of thanks. The witnesses note the example of faith.

The witnesses note Paul giving thanks before all and eating — the example of thanksgiving and calm faith amid the storm.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's giving of thanks and example. The lesson the expositors draw is the example of thanksgiving and calm faith, the apostle owning God even amid the storm. The witnesses note the taking of bread: "he took bread" — Paul took bread; Gill notes this was not the Lord's supper (as some suggested), but common bread such as seafaring men eat; the ordinary meal; the bread taken. The expositors observe the thanksgiving: "gave thanks to God in presence of them all" — Paul gave thanks to God before all, openly; the thanksgiving before the meal; the apostle owning God; the public thanks; the grace before meat. The witnesses observe the leadership by example: Paul led the company not only by word, but by his own example — taking bread, giving thanks, eating; the leader by example; the calm faith communicated; the example that encouraged; the apostle showing the way. The expositors note the calm faith displayed: Paul's calm thanksgiving and eating, amid the storm, displayed his faith — confident in God's promise; the faith shown in the calm; the trust that could give thanks in the tempest; the peace of the believing heart; the faith at rest. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the giving of thanks, the example of faith, and the calm trust amid the storm. The verse thus records Paul's giving of thanks and example — taking bread, giving thanks, eating — teaching the example of thanksgiving and calm faith, the apostle owning God even amid the storm.
36:
"Then were they all of good cheer, and they also took some meat." The witnesses note the company's renewed courage. Ellicott notes the striking contrast to the despair of v.20; the hearty cheerfulness of the apostle had communicated itself to his companions, as by a kind of electric sympathy; they looked to him as their friend and leader, and had spirits to eat once more. The witnesses note the whole company, encouraged by Paul's words and example, taking heart and eating — the contagion of courage from the man of faith; the despair turned to cheer by the apostle's confidence; the leader's faith lifting the hearts of all. The witnesses note the renewed cheer. The witnesses note the contagion of courage.

The witnesses note the company taking heart and eating — the contagion of courage from the man of faith, despair turned to cheer.

So the witnesses make the verse the company's renewed courage. The lesson the expositors draw is the contagion of courage from the man of faith, the despair turned to cheer by the apostle's confidence. The witnesses note the renewed cheer: "then were they all of good cheer" — the whole company took heart; Ellicott notes the striking contrast to the despair of v.20; the despair turned to cheer; the courage renewed; the hearts lifted. The expositors observe Ellicott's note on the communicated cheerfulness: the hearty cheerfulness of the apostle had communicated itself to his companions, as by a kind of electric sympathy; they looked to him as their friend and leader, and had spirits to eat once more; the courage spreading; the leader's faith lifting all. The witnesses press the contagion of courage: Paul's confidence and calm faith spread to the whole company; the contagion of courage; the faith of one lifting the hearts of many; the leader's confidence communicated; the cheer caught from the man of faith. The expositors draw the lesson on the influence of the believer: the calm faith of one godly man lifted the hearts of all the company; the influence of the believer; the courage that spreads; the faith that strengthens others; "thou hast strengthened the weak hands" (Job 4:3); the one man's faith a blessing to many. The witnesses observe the eating: "they also took some meat" — the company, encouraged, took food; the renewed strength; the courage issuing in the practical act; the company fed; the bodies strengthened for the effort. The expositors note the despair turned to cheer: the company, who had given up all hope (v.20), now took heart and ate; the despair turned to cheer; the transformation wrought by the man of faith; the hopelessness turned to hope; the change by the apostle's confidence. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the renewed cheer, the contagion of courage, and the influence of the man of faith. The verse thus records the company's renewed courage — all of good cheer, taking food — teaching the contagion of courage from the man of faith, the despair turned to cheer by the apostle's confidence.
37:
"And we were in all in the ship two hundred threescore and sixteen souls." The witnesses note the number of souls aboard. Ellicott notes the number is given here either as a fact omitted before, or because then for the first time, when they were all gathered at their meal, the writer had taken pains to count them; a man does not commonly count the passengers on a ship until such an occasion. The witnesses note the company numbering two hundred and seventy-six souls — the precise number Luke records, the whole company that God had given to Paul (v.24); the multitude preserved for the sake of the one man of God; the value of the many souls aboard. The witnesses note the number of souls. The witnesses note the company given to Paul.

The witnesses note the 276 souls aboard — the whole company God had given to Paul, the many preserved for the one.

So the witnesses make the verse the number of souls aboard. The lesson the expositors draw is the precise number of the company that God had given to Paul, the many preserved for the sake of the one. The witnesses note the number: "we were in all in the ship two hundred threescore and sixteen souls" — the company numbered 276 souls; Ellicott notes the number is given either as a fact omitted before, or because, when they were all gathered at the meal, the writer counted them; the precise number; the company numbered. The expositors observe the company given to Paul: this was the whole company that God had given to Paul (v.24) — all 276 spared for his sake; the multitude granted to the apostle; the many given to the one; the company preserved. The witnesses press the value of the souls: "two hundred threescore and sixteen souls" — Luke calls them "souls," the precious lives; the many souls aboard; the value of each; the multitude preserved; the souls in God's care. The expositors draw the lesson on the many preserved for the one: 276 lives were spared for the sake of the one man of God; the many preserved for the one righteous; the value of the godly to those around them; "God hath given thee all them that sail with thee" (v.24); the blessing through the one. The witnesses observe the precision of Luke's record: the exact number recorded; the historical precision; the eyewitness detail; the careful record; the accuracy of the account. The expositors note the gathering at the meal: the company, gathered at the meal Paul provided (v.35-36), were counted; the occasion of the counting; the whole company together; the number taken at the meal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the 276 souls, the company given to Paul, and the many preserved for the one. The verse thus records the number of souls aboard — two hundred and seventy-six — teaching the whole company that God had given to Paul, the many preserved for the sake of the one man of God.
38:
"And when they had eaten enough, they lightened the ship, and cast out the wheat into the sea." The witnesses note the final lightening of the ship. Barnes notes that, having no hope of saving the cargo and no further use for it, they threw the wheat into the sea, hoping the ship would draw less water and come nearer the shore. The witnesses note the company, refreshed by the meal, casting out the remaining wheat to lighten the ship for the run to shore — the final preparation for the deliverance; the sacrifice of the last of the cargo, the means employed for the landing; the readiness for the final effort. The witnesses note the casting out of the wheat. The witnesses note the final preparation.

The witnesses note the wheat cast out to lighten the ship — the final preparation, the means employed for the landing.

So the witnesses make the verse the final lightening of the ship. The lesson the expositors draw is the final preparation for the deliverance, the means employed for the landing. The witnesses note the eating: "when they had eaten enough" — the company, refreshed by the meal (v.35-36), had eaten enough; the strength restored; the bodies fortified; the meal taken. The expositors observe the lightening: "they lightened the ship, and cast out the wheat into the sea" — Barnes notes that, having no hope of saving the cargo and no further use for it, they threw the wheat into the sea, hoping the ship would draw less water and come nearer the shore; the final lightening; the wheat cast out; the ship lightened for the run to shore. The witnesses press the final preparation: the casting out of the wheat was the final preparation for the deliverance — to lighten the ship for the run to land; the preparation for the landing; the means for the final effort; the readiness for the deliverance. The expositors draw the lesson on the means employed for the deliverance: the company used every means — eating for strength, lightening the ship — to prepare for the landing; the means employed; the diligent preparation; the effort joined to the promise; the means for the end. The witnesses observe the sacrifice of the last cargo: the wheat (the valuable grain) was cast out; the last of the cargo sacrificed; the goods given up for the deliverance; the cargo abandoned; the sacrifice for survival. The expositors note the strength for the effort: the company, strengthened by the meal, were able to do the work of lightening; the strength restored for the labour; the meal serving the effort; the food enabling the work; the wisdom of Paul's counsel (v.34) shown. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the casting out of the wheat, the final preparation, and the means employed for the landing. The verse thus records the final lightening of the ship — casting out the wheat — teaching the final preparation for the deliverance, the means employed for the landing.
39:
"And when it was day, they knew not the land: but they discovered a certain creek with a shore, into the which they were minded, if it were possible, to thrust in the ship." The witnesses note the sighting of the bay at daybreak. Barnes notes they had been driven by the tempest without observation and were strangers to the coast; they discovered a bay (creek) with a level, accessible shore, into which they purposed to run the ship. The witnesses note the company, at daybreak, not knowing the land but spying a bay with a beach where they hoped to run the ship aground — the dawn revealing the hope of escape; the providence bringing them to a place fit for landing; the day of deliverance come. The witnesses note the unknown land. The witnesses note the bay for landing.

The witnesses note the bay sighted at daybreak — the dawn revealing the hope of escape, the providence bringing them to a fit place.

So the witnesses make the verse the sighting of the bay at daybreak. The lesson the expositors draw is the dawn revealing the hope of escape, the providence bringing them to a place fit for landing. The witnesses note the unknown land: "when it was day, they knew not the land" — Barnes notes they had been driven by the tempest without observation and were strangers to the coast; the unknown shore; the land unrecognized; the place unknown. The expositors observe the bay sighted: "they discovered a certain creek with a shore, into the which they were minded... to thrust in the ship" — they spied a bay (creek) with a level, accessible shore, into which they purposed to run the ship; the place for landing; the beach sighted; the hope of escape. The witnesses press the dawn of deliverance: the day, long longed for (v.29), came and revealed the bay where they might land; the dawn of deliverance; the light revealing the hope; the day come; "weeping may endure for a night, but joy cometh in the morning" (Psalm 30:5). The expositors draw the lesson on the providence bringing them to a fit place: though they knew not the land, the providence had brought them to a place with a shore fit for landing; the providence directing the helpless drift to the right place; the unknown land yet the appointed place; the providence in the landfall. The witnesses observe the hope of the landing: the bay with a beach offered hope of running the ship aground and escaping; the hope of escape; the plan for the landing; the deliverance in view; the shore a hope. The expositors note the human purpose joined to the providence: they "were minded" to run the ship in; the human purpose; the plan employed; the means joined to the providence; the seamanship serving the deliverance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the unknown land, the bay for landing, and the dawn revealing the hope. The verse thus records the sighting of the bay at daybreak — a creek with a shore — teaching the dawn revealing the hope of escape, the providence bringing them to a place fit for landing.
40:
"And when they had taken up the anchors, they committed themselves unto the sea, and loosed the rudder bands, and hoised up the mainsail to the wind, and made toward shore." The witnesses note the final run toward the shore. Barnes notes they cut or slipped the four anchors (leaving them in the sea), loosed the rudder-bands (which had been tied while at anchor), hoisted the foresail to the wind, and made for the shore. The witnesses note the company taking up the anchors, loosing the rudder, hoisting the sail, and steering for the shore — the final, concerted effort for the landing; the diligent use of every means in the run for deliverance; the seamanship employed for the final approach. The witnesses note the run to shore. The witnesses note the final effort.

The witnesses note the final run toward the shore — the concerted effort and diligent use of means for the landing.

So the witnesses make the verse the final run toward the shore. The lesson the expositors draw is the final, concerted effort for the landing, the diligent use of every means. The witnesses note the loosing: "when they had taken up the anchors... and loosed the rudder bands" — Barnes notes they cut or slipped the four anchors (leaving them in the sea) and loosed the rudder-bands (which had been tied while at anchor); the preparation for the run; the ship freed for steering; the loosing for the approach. The expositors observe the hoisting and steering: "hoised up the mainsail to the wind, and made toward shore" — they hoisted the foresail to the wind and made for the shore; the sail set; the ship steered for the beach; the run to land. The witnesses press the concerted effort: the company, with one accord, took up the anchors, loosed the rudder, hoisted the sail, and steered for the shore; the concerted effort; the diligent labour; the final push for the landing; the united work. The expositors draw the lesson on the diligent use of means: the company used every means — the anchors, the rudder, the sail — in the run for deliverance; the diligent seamanship; the means employed to the last; the effort joined to the promise; the diligence in the deliverance. The witnesses observe the commitment to the sea: "they committed themselves unto the sea" — they ventured the run, committing themselves to the sea for the landing; the venture; the committal to the effort; the run undertaken; the bold attempt. The expositors note the harmony of the means and the promise once more: though God had promised deliverance (v.24), the company diligently used every means to reach the shore; the means and the promise joined; the diligence consistent with the assurance; the effort and the faith together. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the run to shore, the final effort, and the diligent use of means. The verse thus records the final run toward the shore — hoisting the sail, steering for land — teaching the final, concerted effort for the landing, the diligent use of every means in the run for deliverance.
41:
"And falling into a place where two seas met, they ran the ship aground; and the forepart stuck fast, and remained unmoveable, but the hinder part was broken with the violence of the waves." The witnesses note the running aground and breaking up of the ship. Barnes notes "a place where two seas met" was a place where the waves dashed from different directions; being carried by wind and waves, the ship ran aground, the bow stuck fast, and the stern was broken by the violence of the waves. The witnesses note the ship running aground at the place where two seas met, the bow fixed fast and the stern breaking under the waves — the wreck of the ship, fulfilling Paul's word (v.22); the vessel lost, yet the company about to be delivered; the loss of the ship and the saving of the souls. The witnesses note the running aground. The witnesses note the breaking of the ship.

The witnesses note the ship running aground and breaking up — the wreck fulfilling Paul's word, the ship lost but the souls to be saved.

So the witnesses make the verse the running aground and breaking up of the ship. The lesson the expositors draw is the wreck of the ship fulfilling Paul's word, the vessel lost yet the company to be delivered. The witnesses note the running aground: "falling into a place where two seas met, they ran the ship aground" — Barnes notes "a place where two seas met" was where the waves dashed from different directions; the ship, carried by wind and waves, ran aground; the grounding; the ship fixed; the vessel stranded. The expositors observe the breaking up: "the forepart stuck fast, and remained unmoveable, but the hinder part was broken with the violence of the waves" — the bow stuck fast, and the stern was broken by the violence of the waves; the ship breaking up; the vessel destroyed; the wreck. The witnesses press the fulfilment of Paul's word: the wreck of the ship fulfilled Paul's word (v.22) — "no loss of any man's life, but of the ship"; the ship lost, as foretold; the prophecy fulfilled; the word confirmed; the loss of the ship foreseen. The expositors draw the lesson on the loss of the ship and the saving of the souls: the ship was lost, but the souls would be saved (v.44); the vessel destroyed, yet all preserved; the loss of the means, the saving of the lives; the deliverance through the wreck; "no loss of any man's life, but of the ship" (v.22). The witnesses observe the violence of the waves: the stern was broken with the violence of the waves; the power of the sea; the destruction of the ship; the waves prevailing over the vessel; the wreck violent. The expositors note the providence in the wreck: the wreck, terrible as it was, was the means of the deliverance — for through it, cast upon the shore, all would be saved; the providence in the wreck; the deliverance through the loss; the wreck serving the salvation; the providence over the destruction. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the running aground, the breaking of the ship, and the wreck fulfilling Paul's word. The verse thus records the running aground and breaking up of the ship — teaching the wreck fulfilling Paul's word, the ship lost yet the company to be delivered through it.
42:
"And the soldiers' counsel was to kill the prisoners, lest any of them should swim out, and escape." The witnesses note the soldiers' cruel counsel. Barnes notes the soldiers' motive was probably the strict Roman military discipline — if the prisoners escaped, the soldiers would be held responsible, and might be charged with negligence; they therefore proposed to kill the prisoners. The witnesses note the soldiers counselling to kill the prisoners (including Paul) lest any escape — the cruel self-interest that would have slain the innocent, even the man of God to whom they owed their lives; the danger to Paul at the very moment of deliverance, the providence about to intervene. The witnesses note the cruel counsel. The witnesses note the danger to Paul.

The witnesses note the soldiers' counsel to kill the prisoners — the cruel self-interest threatening Paul at the moment of deliverance.

So the witnesses make the verse the soldiers' cruel counsel. The lesson the expositors draw is the cruel self-interest that would have slain the innocent, the danger to Paul at the moment of deliverance. The witnesses note the counsel: "the soldiers' counsel was to kill the prisoners, lest any of them should swim out, and escape" — the soldiers counselled to kill the prisoners lest any escape; the cruel counsel; the proposal to slay the prisoners; the deadly advice. The expositors observe the motive: Barnes notes the soldiers' motive was probably the strict Roman military discipline — if the prisoners escaped, the soldiers would be held responsible, and might be charged with negligence (and punished with death, as Acts 12:19); they proposed to kill the prisoners; the self-interest; the fear of the penalty. The witnesses press the cruelty: the soldiers would slay the innocent prisoners — including Paul, the man of God to whom they owed their lives — to save themselves; the cruel self-interest; the slaying of the innocent; the ingratitude; the cruelty of self-preservation. The expositors draw the lesson on the danger to Paul: at the very moment of deliverance, Paul was in danger of being slain by the soldiers; the danger at the brink of safety; the threat to the man of God; the peril at the last; the danger even after the storm. The witnesses observe the ingratitude: the soldiers would slay Paul, for whose sake their lives had been spared (v.24); the ingratitude; the slaying of the deliverer; the cruelty to the benefactor; the self-interest blind to the debt. The expositors note the providence about to intervene: at this moment of danger, the providence would intervene (the centurion sparing Paul, v.43); the providence preserving the apostle; the danger met by the divine protection; the deliverance secured against the threat; the providence over the peril. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the cruel counsel, the danger to Paul, and the providence about to intervene. The verse thus records the soldiers' cruel counsel — to kill the prisoners — teaching the cruel self-interest that would have slain the innocent, the danger to Paul at the moment of deliverance.
43:
"But the centurion, willing to save Paul, kept them from their purpose; and commanded that they which could swim should cast themselves first into the sea, and get to land:" The witnesses note the centurion saving Paul and so all the prisoners. Gill notes the centurion was willing to save Paul, not only as a Roman citizen and an extraordinary person, but chiefly moved by a superior influence, that the will of God might be fulfilled, that Paul should go to Rome. Barnes notes the centurion's kindness, shown from the first (v.3) and confirmed by Paul's conduct and wisdom. The witnesses note the centurion, to save Paul, forbidding the killing and ordering the swimmers to make for land — the providence using the centurion's regard for Paul to save all the prisoners; the man of God the means of the whole company's deliverance. The witnesses note the centurion saving Paul. The witnesses note all saved for Paul's sake.

The witnesses note the centurion saving Paul and so all the prisoners — the providence using his regard for Paul to deliver all.

So the witnesses make the verse the centurion saving Paul and so all. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence using the centurion's regard for Paul to save all the prisoners, the man of God the means of the whole company's deliverance. The witnesses note the centurion's purpose: "the centurion, willing to save Paul, kept them from their purpose" — the centurion, wishing to save Paul, forbade the soldiers' purpose to kill the prisoners; the saving of Paul; the cruel counsel overruled; the apostle protected. The expositors observe the centurion's motive: Gill notes the centurion was willing to save Paul, not only as a Roman citizen and an extraordinary person, but chiefly moved by a superior influence, that the will of God might be fulfilled (that Paul go to Rome); Barnes notes the centurion's kindness, shown from the first (v.3); the regard for Paul; the providence moving the centurion. The witnesses press the saving of all for Paul's sake: to save Paul, the centurion forbade the killing of all the prisoners; so all the prisoners were saved for Paul's sake; the man of God the means of all the prisoners' deliverance; the many spared for the one; the blessing through the one. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence using the centurion: the providence used the centurion's regard for Paul to save all; the divine purpose accomplished through the centurion's kindness; the providence inclining his heart; the will of God fulfilled through the man's regard; the means of the deliverance. The witnesses observe the order to swim: "commanded that they which could swim should cast themselves first into the sea, and get to land" — the centurion ordered the swimmers to make for land; the means of escape; the orderly evacuation; the way to shore; the deliverance begun. The expositors note the value of the godly once more: as the whole company was spared for Paul (v.24), so now the prisoners were saved for his sake; the value of the godly to those around them; the man of God a blessing to all; the one righteous the means of many's deliverance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the centurion saving Paul, all saved for Paul's sake, and the providence using the centurion. The verse thus records the centurion saving Paul and so all the prisoners — teaching the providence using his regard for Paul to deliver all, the man of God the means of the whole company's salvation.
44:
"And the rest, some on boards, and some on broken pieces of the ship. And so it came to pass, that they all escaped safe to land." The witnesses note the safe escape of all to land. Barnes notes the rest (those who could not swim) escaped on boards and broken pieces of the ship; "they escaped all safe to land," according to the promise to Paul (v.22), by the special Providence of God — a remarkable instance of divine interposition to save so many through such dangers, showing that God can defend in any peril and accomplish all his purposes. The witnesses note the whole company — swimmers and the rest on planks — all reaching the shore safe, exactly as God had promised; the complete fulfilment of the promise, not one life lost; the providence saving all, the word of God proved true. The witnesses note the safe escape of all. The witnesses note the promise fulfilled.

The witnesses note all escaping safe to land — the complete fulfilment of the promise, not one life lost, the providence saving all.

So the witnesses make the verse the safe escape of all to land. The lesson the expositors draw is the complete fulfilment of God's promise, not one life lost, the providence saving all. The witnesses note the means of escape: "the rest, some on boards, and some on broken pieces of the ship" — Barnes notes the rest (those who could not swim) escaped on boards and broken pieces of the ship; the means of escape; the planks and fragments; the floating to shore. The expositors observe the complete deliverance: "they all escaped safe to land" — Barnes notes they escaped all safe to land, according to the promise to Paul (v.22), by the special Providence of God; the complete deliverance; all saved; not one lost; the whole company delivered. The witnesses press the fulfilment of the promise: the safe escape of all fulfilled exactly God's promise (v.22, 24) — "no loss of any man's life"; the complete fulfilment; the word of God proved true; not one life lost; the promise kept to the letter. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence saving all: Barnes notes it was a remarkable instance of divine interposition to save so many through such dangers, showing that God can defend in any peril and accomplish all his purposes; the providence saving all; the divine power displayed; "God can defend in any peril"; the providence triumphant; the deliverance complete. The witnesses observe the certainty of God's word proved: the deliverance of all, exactly as promised, proved the certainty of God's word; the word fulfilled; the promise sure; the faith of Paul (v.25, "I believe God") vindicated; the word confirmed by the event. The expositors note the close of the chapter and the providence over the whole: the storm, the wreck, the danger, all overruled to bring the whole company safe to land, and Paul toward Rome; the providence over the whole voyage; the purpose accomplished; "all things work together for good" (Romans 8:28); the providence triumphant. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the safe escape of all, the promise fulfilled, and the providence saving all. The verse thus records the safe escape of all to land — teaching the complete fulfilment of God's promise, not one life lost, the providence saving all and the word of God proved true.
